request.question: Who was the 2nd year head coach of the team who played against Florida State in the 101st Rose Bowl?

title: 1976 Rose Bowl
1995 Rose Bowl
1975 Rose Bowl
1965 Rose Bowl
2013 Michigan State Spartans football team
1954 UCLA Bruins football team
J.K. Kennedy
502nd Infantry Regiment (United States)
2014 Oregon Ducks football team
2015 Rose Bowl
sentences: The 1976 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 1976.
It was the 62nd Rose Bowl Game.
The UCLA Bruins defeated the number one ranked and undefeated Ohio State Buckeyes 23–10 in a rare bowl rematch of a regular season game.
UCLA quarterback John Sciarra was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.

This was the first post season that football teams from the Big Ten Conference and Pacific-10 Conference could appear in other bowl games since the Rose Bowl agreement was signed for the 1947 Rose Bowl.
This game marked Ohio State coach Woody Hayes' last appearance in the Rose Bowl game.
The 1995 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on Monday January 2, 1995, because New Year's Day was on a Sunday.
It was the 81st Rose Bowl Game.
The Penn State Nittany Lions defeated the Oregon Ducks 38–20.
Ki-Jana Carter of Penn State and Danny O'Neil of Oregon both were named the Rose Bowl Players of the Game.
Many observers point to this game as the one that made Ki-Jana Carter a number one selection in the 1995 NFL Draft.
The 1975 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 1975.
It was the 61st Rose Bowl Game.
Fifth ranked USC Trojans defeated third ranked Ohio State Buckeyes 18-17 in one of the most exciting games in the history of the Rose Bowl.
USC quarterback Pat Haden passed to Shelton Diggs for the two-point conversion that gave the Trojans the Rose Bowl victory and the UPI's college football national title.
The 1965 Rose Bowl, played on January 1, 1965, was the 51st Rose Bowl Game.
The Michigan Wolverines defeated the Oregon State Beavers by a score of 34–7.
Michigan fullback Mel Anthony was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.
The 2013 Michigan State Spartans football team represented Michigan State University in the Legends Division of the Big Ten Conference during the 2013 NCAA Division I FBS football season.
Michigan State played their home games at Spartan Stadium in East Lansing, Michigan and were led by seventh year head coach Mark Dantonio.
The Spartans finished the year 13–1, 8–0 and undefeated in Big Ten play.
By winning the Legends Division, they earned a trip to the Big Ten Championship Game, their second appearance in the championship game.
Facing No. 2-ranked Ohio State, they defeated the Buckeyes 34–24, knocking Ohio State out of the running for the BCS National Championship Game.
The Spartans received an invitation to the Rose Bowl, their first trip to the Rose Bowl since 1988.
There they defeated No. 5-ranked Stanford for the school's first Rose Bowl win since 1988.
The 1954 UCLA Bruins football team represented the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) in the 1954 college football season.
They played their home games at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum and were coached by Henry Russell Sanders.
It was Sanders' sixth season as the UCLA head coach.
The Bruins finished 9–0 overall, and were Pacific Coast Conference Champions with a 6–0 record.
The Bruins compiled 367 points against their opponents in nine games, and allowed only 40 points.
The Bruins were not eligible to play in the Rose Bowl vs. Ohio State because of the "no repeat" rule in effect at the time.
No team was allowed to participate in consecutive Rose Bowl games, and since UCLA had played in the 1954 Rose Bowl, they were excluded from the 1955 event.
USC competed in the Rose Bowl instead, losing to Ohio State.
Following the outcome, UCLA and Ohio State split the national championship since UCLA was ranked #1 by the UPI Coaches Poll and Ohio State was ranked #1 by the AP Poll.
Jesse Kennard "Bud" Kennedy was Florida State men's basketball head coach from 1949 to 1966.
He was born on April 22, 1907 in Lawrence, Kansas.
His father, A. R. Kennedy, was head coach of the Kansas football team.
Kennedy was an athlete at University of Kansas, Baker University, and Kansas State Teachers College.
In 1948 he became the Florida State Seminoles Athletic Manager and Assistant head coach.
The following year, he became the head coach.
Kennedy went 234–208 in his 18 years at Florida State and won the Florida Intercollegiate Conference Championship in 1955.
In 1966, on June, 26 he died of cancer.
Kennedy was 59.
Later in 1977 he was inducted into the Florida State University Hall of Fame.
The 502nd Infantry Regiment (502nd INF), previously titled the 502nd Parachute Infantry Regiment (502nd PIR), is an airborne infantry regiment of the United States Army.

The regiment was established shortly after the American entry into World War II, and was assigned as a regiment of the 101st Airborne Division, "The Screaming Eagles", one of the most decorated formations of the U.S. Army.
The regiment saw substantial action in the European Theater of World War II and was deactivated in 1945, shortly after the end of the war.

Reactivating in a new form in 1956, the 502nd Infantry has served in the Vietnam War, the first Gulf War, Operation Iraqi Freedom, Operation Enduring Freedom in Afghanistan, and Operation Inherent Resolve in Iraq.
It was removed from parachute status with the rest of the 101st Airborne Division in 1969, and since 1974, the regiment has been classified as an Air Assault unit.
Currently, its 1st and 2nd battalions are active.
Both battalions are assigned to the 2nd Brigade Combat Team, 101st Airborne Division (Air Assault).
The 2014 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2014 NCAA Division I FBS football season.
The team was led by second-year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 48th straight year.
They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

The 2015 Rose Bowl (officially known as the College Football Playoff Semifinal at the Rose Bowl Game presented by Northwestern Mutual) was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 2015, at the Rose Bowl stadium in Pasadena, California.

This 101st Rose Bowl Game, as a semifinal for the College Football Playoff (CFP), matched the Oregon Ducks against the Florida State Seminoles as selected by the system's selection committee to compete for a spot at the National Championship game to be played on January 12, 2015 at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.
It was one of the 2014–15 bowl games that concluded the 2014 FBS football season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Who was the 2nd year head coach of the team who played against Florida State in the 101st Rose Bowl?
hop1_docs: 1957 Stanford Indians football team | The 1957 Stanford Indians football team represented Stanford University in the 1957 college football season.  The team was coached by Chuck Taylor in his seventh year as head coach.  Taylor was a popular coach who had led the Indians to the 1952 Rose Bowl and who had been an All-American player for the Indians who starred on the undefeated 1940 "Wow Boys" team that won the 1941 Rose Bowl and the national championship in several polls.  Just before the team's final game against archrival California, Taylor announced he was retiring as head coach to become assistant athletic director of the university.

1999 Michigan State Spartans football team | The 1999 Michigan State Spartans football team represented Michigan State University in the 1999 NCAA Division I-A football season season.  The Spartans played their home games at Spartan Stadium in East Lansing, Michigan.  This was the last year for head coach Nick Saban, who left the program on December 5 to take the head coaching position at Louisiana State.  During the bowl game, the Spartans were coached by interim head coach Bobby Williams, who led the Spartans to a 37–34 victory in the 2000 Florida Citrus Bowl over the Florida Gators of the Southeastern Conference, with a last second, game-winning field goal by kicker Paul Edinger.

2015 Rose Bowl | The 2015 Rose Bowl (officially known as the College Football Playoff Semifinal at the Rose Bowl Game presented by Northwestern Mutual) was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 2015, at the Rose Bowl stadium in Pasadena, California.  This 101st Rose Bowl Game, as a semifinal for the College Football Playoff (CFP), matched the Oregon Ducks against the Florida State Seminoles as selected by the system's selection committee to compete for a spot at the National Championship game to be played on January 12, 2015 at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.  It was one of the 2014–15 bowl games that concluded the 2014 FBS football season.

2016 South Florida Bulls football team | The 2016 South Florida Bulls football team represented the University of South Florida (USF) in the 2016 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The 2016 season is the 20th season for the Bulls, and their fourth as a member of the American Athletic Conference (The American).  They played their home games at Raymond James Stadium in Tampa, Florida, and were led during the regular season by head coach Willie Taggart, who was in his fourth year.  Following the regular season, but before the team's appearance in the Birmingham Bowl, Taggart left to take the head coaching vacancy at Oregon, with co-offensive coordinator T. J. Weist named as interim head coach for the bowl game.

1986 Indiana Hoosiers football team | The 1986 Indiana Hoosiers football team represented Indiana University during the 1986 NCAA Division I-A football season.  They participated as members of the Big Ten Conference.  The Hoosiers played their home games at Memorial Stadium in Bloomington, Indiana.  The team was led by Bill Mallory, who was serving in his third year as head coach of the Hoosiers.  The Hoosiers participated in the All-American Bowl against Florida State, losing 13–27.

1984 Rose Bowl | The 1984 Rose Bowl game, played on January 2, was the 70th Rose Bowl Game.  The UCLA Bruins defeated the Illinois Fighting Illini by a score of 45–9.  Rick Neuheisel, UCLA quarterback, was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.  He completed 22 of 32 passes for 298 yards and four touchdowns. Neuheisel, who was named head coach of Bruins in December 2007, threw two touchdown passes to his eventual predecessor as Bruin head coach, Karl Dorrell.  The game was played on January 2, as New Year's Day fell on a Sunday.

1996 Ohio State Buckeyes football team | The 1996 Ohio State Buckeyes football team represented the Ohio State University in the 1996 NCAA Division I-A football season.  The team's head coach was John Cooper.  The Buckeyes played their home games in Ohio Stadium.  The team finished the season with a win-loss record of 11–1, and a Big Ten Conference record of 7–1.  They were co-champions of Big Ten Conference with the Northwestern Wildcats and played in the 1997 Rose Bowl against Pacific-10 Confernece champion the Arizona State Sun Devils.  Ohio State did not play Northwestern during the regular season and were selected to play in the Rose Bowl due to their better overall record of 10–1 (the Wildcats were 9–2).  Northwestern had played in the previous Rose Bowl.
response.summary_1: Passage [3] states the 101st Rose Bowl "matched the Oregon Ducks against the Florida State Seminoles" (the 2015 Rose Bowl played on January 1, 2015). Missing fact/bridge: the identity of the Oregon Ducks' head coach for that game/season and whether he was in his 2nd year. Retrieval directions/anchors: search for the 2014 Oregon Ducks football team or "Oregon Ducks head coach 2014" (look for named head coach + "second year" or "in his second year" phrasing, or the coach's tenure year next to the 2014 season).
response.hop2_query: 2014 Oregon Ducks head coach second year
hop2_docs: 2014 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2014 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2014 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by second-year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 48th straight year.  They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

2014–15 Oregon Ducks men's basketball team | The 2014–15 Oregon Ducks men's basketball team represented the University of Oregon during the 2014–15 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  The Ducks were led by their fifth year head coach Dana Altman.  They played their home games at Matthew Knight Arena and were members of the Pac-12 Conference.  They finished the season 26–10, 13–5 in Pac-12 play to finish in a tie for second place.  They advanced to the championship game of the Pac-12 Tournament where they lost to Arizona.  They received an at-large bid to the NCAA Tournament where they defeated Oklahoma State in the second round before losing in the third round to Wisconsin.

2009 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2009 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2009 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by head coach Chip Kelly in his first season as a head coach at the Division I FBS level.  Kelly was only the third Ducks head coach since 1977 and led the Ducks to a Pac-10 Championship and was named Pac-10 Coach of the Year.  He took over for Mike Bellotti.

2010 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2010 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2010 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by Chip Kelly in his second season as a head coach.  The Ducks played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 44th straight year.

2014–15 Oregon Ducks women's basketball team | The 2014–15 Oregon Ducks women's basketball team represented the University of Oregon during the 2014–15 NCAA Division I women's basketball season.  The Ducks, led by first year head coach Kelly Graves, played their games at the Matthew Knight Arena and were members of the Pac-12 Conference.  They finished the season 13–17, 6–12 in Pac-12 play to finish in a tie for ninth place.  They lost in the first round in the Pac-12 Women's Tournament to Washington State.

2013–14 Oregon Ducks women's basketball team | The 2013–14 Oregon Ducks women's basketball team represented the University of Oregon during the 2013–14 NCAA Division I women's basketball season.  The Ducks, led by fifth year head coach Paul Westhead, played their games at the Matthew Knight Arena and were members of the Pac-12 Conference.  They finished with a record of 16–16 overall, 6–12 in Pac-12 play for a tie for a ninth-place finish.  They lost in the first round of the 2014 Pac-12 Conference Women's Basketball Tournament to Washington State.  They were invited to the 2014 Women's National Invitation Tournament which they defeated Pacific in the first round before losing to Washington in the second round.

1995 Oregon Ducks football team | The 1995 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon during the 1995 NCAA Division I-A football season.  They were led by head coach Mike Bellotti, who was in his 1st season as head coach of the Ducks after replacing Rich Brooks, who resigned in February 1995 to become the head coach of the St. Louis Rams.  They played their home games at Autzen Stadium in Eugene, Oregon and participated as members of the Pacific-10 Conference.
response.summary_2: The Oregon Ducks were coached by Mark Helfrich, who was in his second year as head coach for the 2014 season. Passage [1] explicitly confirms the 2014 Oregon Ducks were "led by second-year head coach Mark Helfrich."
